item.title: South Korea's Gwangju Inhwa School Abused Deaf Students for Decades
item.description: Gwangju Inhwa School, a South Korean institution for hearing-impaired students, was found in 2005 to have subjected disabled children to years of physical and sexual abuse by staff and administrators between 2000 and 2003. Initial trials drew little attention and resulted in lenient sentences, including suspended terms for some convicted perpetrators. The 2011 film Silenced, based on a novel about the case, was seen by nearly five percent of South Korea's population and reignited public outrage, prompting a police reinvestigation. The school was closed in November 2011, and South Korea passed the Dogani Bill, eliminating the statute of limitations for such crimes against children and disabled women.

media:content.media:title: South Korea's Gwangju Inhwa School Abused Deaf Students for Decades
media:content.media:description: Gwangju Inhwa School, a South Korean institution for hearing-impaired students, was found in 2005 to have subjected disabled children to years of physical and sexual abuse by staff and administrators between 2000 and 2003. Initial trials drew little attention and resulted in lenient sentences, including suspended terms for some convicted perpetrators. The 2011 film Silenced, based on a novel about the case, was seen by nearly five percent of South Korea's population and reignited public outrage, prompting a police reinvestigation. The school was closed in November 2011, and South Korea passed the Dogani Bill, eliminating the statute of limitations for such crimes against children and disabled women.
